content:
# Exodus Chapter 18 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~43v–44r (PDF pp. 82–83); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 59–60 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_018_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_018_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT OBSERVATION — JETHRO'S VISIT AND THE BIRTH OF JUDICIAL ADMINISTRATION:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 18 in standard prose column format across two pages. This chapter stands as a remarkable interlude between the wilderness journey narratives (chs. 15–17) and the Sinai theophany (chs. 19–20). It is the only chapter in the Torah where a non-Israelite — Yitro (Jethro), priest of Midyan — initiates a major theological confession and institutional reform within Yisra'el. The chapter divides into three movements: the reunion of Mosheh's family (vv. 1–6), Yitro's confession of YHWH's supremacy over all gods (vv. 7–12), and the establishment of a tiered judicial system (vv. 13–27). Yitro's confession in vv. 10–11 — "Now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods" — is one of the most significant monotheistic declarations by a non-Israelite in the Hebrew Bible. The judicial reform in vv. 21–22 establishes the four-tier system of sarei alafim, sarei me'ot, sarei khamishim, and sarei asarot (commanders of thousands, hundreds, fifties, and tens) that becomes foundational for Yisra'el's governance. The Vaticanus text (pp. 59–60) presents the LXX rendering in continuous uncial script; the chapter heading is visible at the transition point on page 59, with the bulk of chapter 18 continuing onto page 60.

---

## Jethro Brings Mosheh's Family (vv. 1–6)

**1** And Yitro [Jethro], priest [kohen] of Midyan, father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh, heard [vayishma] all [et-kol] that Elohim [God] had done [asah] for Mosheh and for Yisra'el his people [ammo] — that YHWH had brought out [hotsi] Yisra'el from Mitsrayim [Egypt].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּשְׁמַע יִתְרוֹ כֹהֵן מִדְיָן חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה אֱלֹהִים לְמֹשֶׁה וּלְיִשְׂרָאֵל עַמּוֹ כִּי־הוֹצִיא יְהוָה אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִמִּצְרָיִם (vayishma Yitro kohen Midyan khoten Mosheh et kol-asher asah Elohim le-Mosheh u-le-Yisra'el ammo ki-hotsi YHWH et-Yisra'el mi-Mitsrayim, "and Yitro, priest of Midyan, father-in-law of Mosheh, heard all that Elohim had done for Mosheh and for Yisra'el his people — that YHWH had brought out Yisra'el from Mitsrayim"). **Key terms:** יִתְרוֹ (Yitro) = "Jethro" — the name likely derives from the root יתר (yatar, "to remain over, to be in excess, to excel"), suggesting "his excellence" or "his abundance." He is also called רְעוּאֵל (Re'u'el, "friend/shepherd of God," Ex 2:18) and in some traditions חוֹבָב (Khovav, Num 10:29; Judg 4:11). The multiple names may reflect different traditions or different titles. כֹהֵן (kohen) = "priest" — from a root meaning "to stand, minister, serve." Yitro is a priest of Midyan, a non-Israelite cultic leader. His exact religious role is debated: was he a priest of YHWH already (since Midyan is near Sinai and the burning bush encounter occurred in his territory), or a pagan priest who converts upon hearing of YHWH's deeds? The text leaves this ambiguous. חֹתֵן (khoten) = "father-in-law" — specifically the wife's father. This term defines Yitro's relationship to Mosheh through Tsipporah. The verse uses both divine names: אֱלֹהִים (Elohim, "God," the generic/universal name) in the first clause and יְהוָה (YHWH, the covenant name) in the second. Yitro hears what Elohim (God generically) did; the narrator specifies that it was YHWH (the covenant God) who brought Yisra'el out. LXX: καὶ ἤκουσεν Ιοθορ ὁ ἱερεὺς Μαδιαμ ὁ γαμβρὸς Μωυσῆ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησεν κύριος Ισραηλ τῷ ἑαυτοῦ λαῷ· ἐξήγαγεν γὰρ κύριος τὸν Ισραηλ ἐξ Αἰγύπτου ("and Iothor the priest of Madiam, the father-in-law of Mosheh, heard all the things that the Lord did for Israel his people; for the Lord led Israel out of Egypt") — the LXX uses κύριος ("Lord") for both divine names, losing the distinction between Elohim and YHWH; γαμβρός ("father-in-law, kinsman by marriage") for khoten.

**2** And Yitro, father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh, took [vayiqqakh] Tsipporah [Zipporah], wife [eshet] of Mosheh, after [akhar] her sending away [shillukheha];

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקַּח יִתְרוֹ חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה אֶת־צִפֹּרָה אֵשֶׁת מֹשֶׁה אַחַר שִׁלּוּחֶיהָ (vayiqqakh Yitro khoten Mosheh et-Tsipporah eshet Mosheh akhar shillukheha, "and Yitro, father-in-law of Mosheh, took Tsipporah, wife of Mosheh, after her sending away"). **Key terms:** צִפֹּרָה (Tsipporah) = "bird" — from צִפּוֹר (tsippor, "bird"). Mosheh's wife, daughter of Yitro, whom he married after fleeing Egypt to Midyan (Ex 2:21). שִׁלּוּחֶיהָ (shillukheha) = "her sending away" — from שׁלח (shalakh, "to send, send away, let go") in the piel intensive form, + 3fs suffix. The noun שִׁלּוּחִים (shillukhim) can mean "sending away, dismissal" and is used technically for divorce (cf. Deut 24:1, 3 where the related כְּרִיתֻת שִׁלּוּחִים / סֵפֶר כְּרִיתֻת is a "document of cutting off / bill of divorce"). The use of this term here is deliberately ambiguous — did Mosheh formally divorce Tsipporah, or merely send her back to her father for safekeeping during the dangerous mission to Egypt? Most commentators favor the latter: Mosheh sent her back (perhaps after the circumcision incident of 4:24–26) rather than divorcing her. The text does not explain when or why this sending away occurred. LXX: καὶ ἔλαβεν Ιοθορ ὁ γαμβρὸς Μωυσῆ Σεπφωραν τὴν γυναῖκα Μωυσῆ μετὰ τὴν ἄφεσιν αὐτῆς ("and Iothor the father-in-law of Mosheh took Sepphoura the wife of Mosheh after her release/sending away") — ἄφεσις ("release, sending away, dismissal") for shillukheha.

**3** And her two [shenei] sons [vaneha] — of whom the name [shem] of the one [ha-ekhad] [was] Gershom [Sojourner There], for he said: "I was [hayiti] a sojourner [ger] in a foreign [nokhriyyah] land [be-erets]";

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאֵת שְׁנֵי בָנֶיהָ אֲשֶׁר שֵׁם הָאֶחָד גֵּרְשֹׁם כִּי אָמַר גֵּר הָיִיתִי בְּאֶרֶץ נָכְרִיָּה (ve-et shenei vaneha asher shem ha-ekhad Gershom ki amar ger hayiti be-erets nokhriyyah, "and her two sons — of whom the name of the one [was] Gershom, for he said: 'I was a sojourner in a foreign land'"). **Key terms:** גֵּרְשֹׁם (Gershom) = the name is a wordplay on גֵּר (ger, "sojourner, resident alien") + שָׁם (sham, "there"), yielding "a sojourner there." The naming was first given at Ex 2:22, where Mosheh named his firstborn after his experience of exile. The ger is one who resides in a land not his own, without the full rights of a native citizen — Mosheh was a ger in Midyan, an alien in a foreign land. נָכְרִיָּה (nokhriyyah) = "foreign, strange" — feminine adjective from נכר (nakhar, "to be foreign, to recognize as strange"). The word carries connotations of alienation and non-belonging. LXX: καὶ τοὺς δύο υἱοὺς αὐτῆς, ὧν ὄνομα τῷ ἑνὶ Γηρσαμ λέγων· πάροικος ἤμην ἐν γῇ ἀλλοτρίᾳ ("and her two sons, of whom the name of the one [was] Gersam, saying: 'I was a sojourner in a foreign land'") — πάροικος ("sojourner, resident alien") for ger; ἀλλοτρία ("foreign, belonging to another") for nokhriyyah.

**4** And the name [ve-shem] of the other [ha-ekhad] [was] Eli'ezer [My God is Help], "for the Elohei [God of] my father [avi] [was] my help [be-ezri], and he delivered me [vayatssileni] from the kherev [sword] of Par'oh [Pharaoh]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְשֵׁם הָאֶחָד אֱלִיעֶזֶר כִּי אֱלֹהֵי אָבִי בְּעֶזְרִי וַיַּצִּלֵנִי מֵחֶרֶב פַּרְעֹה (ve-shem ha-ekhad Eli'ezer ki Elohei avi be-ezri vayatssileni me-kherev Par'oh, "and the name of the other [was] Eli'ezer, 'for the God of my father [was] my help, and he delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh'"). **Key terms:** אֱלִיעֶזֶר (Eli'ezer) = "My God is help" — from אֵל (El, "God") + עֶזְרָה/עֵזֶר (ezrah/ezer, "help, assistance"). This is the only place in Exodus where the second son is named; he was not mentioned at the birth narrative in 2:22. Some scholars argue that the Eli'ezer naming tradition was originally separate. The name commemorates Mosheh's escape from Pharaoh's attempt to kill him (Ex 2:15). בְּעֶזְרִי (be-ezri) = "in my help / [was] my help" — from עֵזֶר (ezer, "help") + 1cs suffix. חֶרֶב (kherev) = "sword" — the same word used in 17:13 for the sword of battle. Pharaoh's kherev represents the death sentence Mosheh fled from after killing the Egyptian (2:14–15). LXX: καὶ τὸ ὄνομα τοῦ δευτέρου Ελιεζερ λέγων· ὁ γὰρ θεὸς τοῦ πατρός μου βοηθός μου καὶ ἐξείλατό με ἐκ χειρὸς Φαραω ("and the name of the second [was] Eliezer, saying: 'for the God of my father [was] my helper, and he delivered me from the hand of Pharaoh'") — the LXX reads "from the hand of Pharaoh" (ἐκ χειρὸς Φαραω) rather than "from the sword of Pharaoh" (me-kherev Par'oh) — a notable variant: χείρ ("hand") for kherev ("sword").

**5** And Yitro, father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh, came [vayavo] — and his sons [u-vanav] and his wife [ve-ishto] — to Mosheh, to the midbar [wilderness] where he [was] encamping [khoneh] there [sham], [at the] mountain [har] of ha-Elohim [God].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹא יִתְרוֹ חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה וּבָנָיו וְאִשְׁתּוֹ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הַמִּדְבָּר אֲשֶׁר־הוּא חֹנֶה שָׁם הַר הָאֱלֹהִים (vayavo Yitro khoten Mosheh u-vanav ve-ishto el-Mosheh el-ha-midbar asher-hu khoneh sham har ha-Elohim, "and Yitro, father-in-law of Mosheh, came — and his sons and his wife — to Mosheh, to the wilderness where he [was] encamping there, [at the] mountain of God"). **Key terms:** The "sons" (banav) and "wife" (ishto) here refer to Mosheh's sons and wife, whom Yitro is bringing. The pronoun suffixes are on Yitro as subject but refer to the family members he carries. הַר הָאֱלֹהִים (har ha-Elohim) = "the mountain of God" — this is Khorev/Sinai (cf. 3:1; 4:27; 24:13). The chapter places the camp at the mountain of God, the very location of the burning bush and the impending covenant revelation. חֹנֶה (khoneh) = qal active participle of חנה (khanah, "to encamp, camp, pitch tent") — "encamping." The participle indicates an ongoing state: Mosheh was camping there (not just passing through). LXX: καὶ ἐξῆλθεν Ιοθορ ὁ γαμβρὸς Μωυσῆ καὶ οἱ υἱοὶ καὶ ἡ γυνὴ πρὸς Μωυσῆν εἰς τὴν ἔρημον, οὗ παρενέβαλεν ἐπ᾽ ὄρους τοῦ θεοῦ ("and Iothor the father-in-law of Mosheh went out, and the sons and the wife, to Mosheh, into the wilderness where he encamped at the mountain of God") — ἐξέρχομαι ("go out") for vayavo; ὄρος τοῦ θεοῦ ("mountain of God") for har ha-Elohim.

**6** And he said to Mosheh: "I [ani], your father-in-law [khotenkha] Yitro, [am] coming [ba] to you [elekha], and your wife [ve-ishtekha] and her two [shenei] sons [vaneha] with her [immah]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר אֶל־מֹשֶׁה אֲנִי חֹתֶנְךָ יִתְרוֹ בָּא אֵלֶיךָ וְאִשְׁתְּךָ וּשְׁנֵי בָנֶיהָ עִמָּהּ (vayomer el-Mosheh ani khotenkha Yitro ba elekha ve-ishtekha u-shenei vaneha immah, "and he said to Mosheh: 'I, your father-in-law Yitro, [am] coming to you, and your wife and her two sons with her'"). **Key terms:** This verse likely represents a message sent ahead (by messenger) announcing the arrival, rather than a face-to-face statement, since v. 7 describes Mosheh going out to meet Yitro — Mosheh would not go out to meet someone already standing before him. The announcement is formal: "I, your father-in-law Yitro" — a self-identification formula. בָּא (ba) = qal active participle of בוא (bo, "to come, enter") — "coming, about to come." The participle indicates imminent arrival. עִמָּהּ (immah) = "with her" — the sons are described as being "with her" (Tsipporah), emphasizing that the mother has been caring for them during the separation from Mosheh. LXX: καὶ ἀνηγγέλη Μωυσῇ λέγοντες· ἰδοὺ ὁ γαμβρός σου Ιοθορ παραγίνεται πρὸς σέ, καὶ ἡ γυνή σου καὶ οἱ δύο υἱοί σου μετ᾽ αὐτῆς ("and it was announced to Mosheh, saying: 'Behold, your father-in-law Iothor is arriving to you, and your wife and your two sons with her'") — **LXX INTERPRETIVE SHIFT:** The LXX changes the first-person announcement ("I, your father-in-law") to a third-person report: "it was announced to Mosheh" (ἀνηγγέλη, passive) — making explicit the messenger intermediary that the Hebrew only implies. Also, the LXX reads "your two sons" (οἱ δύο υἱοί σου) rather than "her two sons" (shenei vaneha) — a shift from maternal to paternal connection.

---

## Jethro's Confession of YHWH's Supremacy (vv. 7–12)

**7** And Mosheh went out [vayetse] to meet [liqrat] his father-in-law [khotno], and he bowed down [vayishtakhu] and he kissed him [vayishshaq-lo]; and they asked [vayish'alu] each man [ish] his companion [le-re'ehu] of peace [le-shalom]; and they came [vayavo'u] into the tent [ha-ohelah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּצֵא מֹשֶׁה לִקְרַאת חֹתְנוֹ וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ וַיִּשַּׁק־לוֹ וַיִּשְׁאֲלוּ אִישׁ־לְרֵעֵהוּ לְשָׁלוֹם וַיָּבֹאוּ הָאֹהֱלָה (vayetse Mosheh liqrat khotno vayishtakhu vayishshaq-lo vayish'alu ish-le-re'ehu le-shalom vayavo'u ha-ohelah, "and Mosheh went out to meet his father-in-law, and he bowed down and he kissed him; and they asked each man his companion of peace; and they came into the tent"). **Key terms:** וַיִּשְׁתַּחוּ (vayishtakhu) = hishtaphel (reflexive) of שׁחה (shakhah, "to bow down, prostrate oneself") — "he bowed down, he prostrated himself." This is the standard verb for worship/obeisance in the Hebrew Bible. Mosheh bows before his father-in-law — an act of deep respect and familial honor. The leader of Yisra'el, fresh from victories over Egypt and Amaleq, bows to the Midyanite priest. וַיִּשַּׁק (vayishshaq) = qal of נשׁק (nashaq, "to kiss") — "he kissed him." The kiss of greeting between kinsmen (cf. Gen 29:11, 13; 33:4; 45:15). אִישׁ־לְרֵעֵהוּ (ish-le-re'ehu) = "each man to his companion" — the idiom for reciprocal action. רֵעַ (re'a) = "friend, companion, neighbor." לְשָׁלוֹם (le-shalom) = "of peace, concerning well-being" — from שָׁלוֹם (shalom, "peace, wholeness, well-being, completeness"), from שׁלם (shalam, "to be complete, whole, sound"). "Asking of shalom" is the standard greeting — inquiring after someone's wholeness and well-being. הָאֹהֱלָה (ha-ohelah) = "into the tent" — the directional he (ה-) suffix on אֹהֶל (ohel, "tent") indicates motion toward. This is likely Mosheh's tent. LXX: ἐξῆλθεν δὲ Μωυσῆς εἰς συνάντησιν τῷ γαμβρῷ αὐτοῦ καὶ προσεκύνησεν αὐτῷ καὶ ἐφίλησεν αὐτόν, καὶ ἠσπάσαντο ἀλλήλους· καὶ εἰσήγαγεν αὐτὸν εἰς τὴν σκηνήν ("and Mosheh went out to meet his father-in-law and bowed down to him and kissed him, and they greeted one another; and he brought him into the tent") — προσκυνέω ("bow down, worship") for vayishtakhu; φιλέω ("kiss, love") for vayishshaq; ἀσπάζομαι ("greet") for vayish'alu le-shalom.

**8** And Mosheh recounted [vayesapper] to his father-in-law [le-khotno] all [et-kol] that YHWH had done [asah] to Par'oh [Pharaoh] and to Mitsrayim [Egypt] on account of [al-odot] Yisra'el — all [et-kol] the hardship [ha-tela'ah] that had found them [metsa'atam] on the way [ba-derekh] — and YHWH delivered them [vayatssilem].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְסַפֵּר מֹשֶׁה לְחֹתְנוֹ אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה יְהוָה לְפַרְעֹה וּלְמִצְרַיִם עַל אוֹדֹת יִשְׂרָאֵל אֵת כָּל־הַתְּלָאָה אֲשֶׁר מְצָאָתַם בַּדֶּרֶךְ וַיַּצִּלֵם יְהוָה (vayesapper Mosheh le-khotno et kol-asher asah YHWH le-Par'oh u-le-Mitsrayim al odot Yisra'el et kol-ha-tela'ah asher metsa'atam ba-derekh vayatssilem YHWH, "and Mosheh recounted to his father-in-law all that YHWH had done to Pharaoh and to Mitsrayim on account of Yisra'el — all the hardship that had found them on the way — and YHWH delivered them"). **Key terms:** וַיְסַפֵּר (vayesapper) = piel of ספר (safar, "to count, recount, tell, narrate") — "he recounted, he narrated." The piel intensive form suggests a thorough, detailed recounting. This is the oral transmission of the exodus narrative — Mosheh tells the whole story to his father-in-law. עַל אוֹדֹת (al odot) = "on account of, for the sake of, concerning" — from אוֹדוֹת (odot, "causes, circumstances"), possibly from אוֹד (od, a rare noun). תְּלָאָה (tela'ah) = "hardship, weariness, distress" — from לאה (la'ah, "to be weary, to toil"). This word is rare (cf. Num 20:14; Neh 9:32; Lam 3:5) and captures the cumulative exhaustion and difficulty of the wilderness journey. מְצָאָתַם (metsa'atam) = qal perfect 3fs of מצא (matsa, "to find, encounter") + 3mp suffix — "that found them, that befell them." Hardship "finds" people — it comes upon them as an external force. וַיַּצִּלֵם (vayatssilem) = hiphil of נצל (natsal, "to deliver, rescue, snatch away") + 3mp suffix — "and he delivered them." The verb natsal implies snatching from danger, pulling from the grip of a predator. LXX: διηγήσατο δὲ Μωυσῆς τῷ γαμβρῷ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίησεν κύριος τῷ Φαραω καὶ τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις ἕνεκεν τοῦ Ισραηλ καὶ πάντα τὸν μόχθον τὸν γενόμενον αὐτοῖς ἐν τῇ ὁδῷ καὶ ὅτι ἐξείλατο αὐτοὺς κύριος ἐκ χειρὸς Φαραω καὶ ἐκ χειρὸς τῶν Αἰγυπτίων ("and Mosheh related to his father-in-law all the things that the Lord did to Pharaoh and the Egyptians for the sake of Israel, and all the toil that befell them on the way, and that the Lord delivered them from the hand of Pharaoh and from the hand of the Egyptians") — **LXX ADDITION:** The LXX expands the ending with "from the hand of Pharaoh and from the hand of the Egyptians" — not in the MT. μόχθος ("toil, hardship") for tela'ah; διηγέομαι ("relate, narrate") for vayesapper.

**9** And Yitro rejoiced [vayikhad] over [al] all [kol] the good [ha-tovah] that YHWH had done [asah] for Yisra'el, that he had delivered them [hittsilo] from the hand [mi-yad] of Mitsrayim [Egypt].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּחַדְּ יִתְרוֹ עַל כָּל־הַטּוֹבָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה יְהוָה לְיִשְׂרָאֵל אֲשֶׁר הִצִּילוֹ מִיַּד מִצְרָיִם (vayikhad Yitro al kol-ha-tovah asher asah YHWH le-Yisra'el asher hittsilo mi-yad Mitsrayim, "and Yitro rejoiced over all the good that YHWH had done for Yisra'el, that he had delivered them from the hand of Mitsrayim"). **Key terms:** וַיִּחַדְּ (vayikhad) = this verb is a hapax legomenon — it occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its meaning is debated. Most derive it from חדה (khadah, "to rejoice, be glad"), yielding "and he rejoiced." However, Rashi, citing the Talmud (Sanhedrin 94a), notes an alternative: the root may relate to חִדּוּדִים (khiddudim, "prickles, goosebumps"), suggesting that "his flesh became prickly" — i.e., Yitro had a visceral, physical response. The Talmudic reading suggests ambivalence: Yitro rejoiced for Yisra'el but felt a twinge of distress for the Egyptians (as a former pagan). Most modern translations simply render "rejoiced." טוֹבָה (tovah) = "good, goodness, benefit" — feminine of טוֹב (tov, "good"). The "good" (tovah) that YHWH did is the totality of His saving acts. הִצִּילוֹ (hittsilo) = hiphil of נצל (natsal, "to deliver") + 3ms suffix (collective for Yisra'el) — "that he delivered him/them." LXX: ἐξέστη δὲ Ιοθορ ἐπὶ πᾶσιν τοῖς ἀγαθοῖς οἷς ἐποίησεν αὐτοῖς κύριος, ὅτι ἐξείλατο αὐτοὺς ἐκ χειρὸς Αἰγυπτίων καὶ ἐκ χειρὸς Φαραω ("and Iothor was amazed at all the good things which the Lord did for them, that he delivered them from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of Pharaoh") — **LXX INTERPRETIVE SHIFT:** ἐξίστημι ("be amazed, be astonished, be beside oneself") for vayikhad — the LXX reads the reaction as amazement/astonishment rather than joy, perhaps reflecting awareness of the ambiguity in the Hebrew. The LXX again expands with "from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of Pharaoh."

**10** And Yitro said: "Barukh [Blessed be] YHWH who delivered [hittsil] you from the hand [mi-yad] of Mitsrayim and from the hand [u-mi-yad] of Par'oh, who delivered [hittsil] the people [ha-am] from under [mi-takhat] the hand [yad] of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יִתְרוֹ בָּרוּךְ יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר הִצִּיל אֶתְכֶם מִיַּד מִצְרַיִם וּמִיַּד פַּרְעֹה אֲשֶׁר הִצִּיל אֶת־הָעָם מִתַּחַת יַד־מִצְרָיִם (vayomer Yitro barukh YHWH asher hittsil etkhem mi-yad Mitsrayim u-mi-yad Par'oh asher hittsil et-ha-am mi-takhat yad-Mitsrayim, "and Yitro said: 'Blessed be YHWH who delivered you from the hand of Mitsrayim and from the hand of Pharaoh, who delivered the people from under the hand of Mitsrayim'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — Yitro's berakhah (blessing):** בָּרוּךְ (barukh) = "blessed" — qal passive participle of ברך (barakh, "to bless, kneel"). This is the berakhah formula: "Blessed be YHWH who..." — a declaration that YHWH is the source of blessing and worthy of praise. A non-Israelite priest pronounces a berakhah over the God of Yisra'el. This parallels Melchizedek's blessing in Gen 14:19–20 ("Blessed be Avram by El Elyon... and blessed be El Elyon who delivered your enemies into your hand"). The double use of הִצִּיל (hittsil, "delivered") creates emphasis: YHWH delivered both the leaders ("you," plural) and the people as a whole. מִתַּחַת יַד (mi-takhat yad) = "from under the hand of" — the preposition מִתַּחַת (mi-takhat, "from under, from beneath") + יַד (yad, "hand") creates the image of Yisra'el trapped beneath the crushing hand of Egypt, from which YHWH pulled them free. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν Ιοθορ· εὐλογητὸς κύριος, ὅτι ἐξείλατο αὐτοὺς ἐκ χειρὸς Αἰγυπτίων καὶ ἐκ χειρὸς Φαραω ("and Iothor said: 'Blessed be the Lord, for he delivered them from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of Pharaoh'") — εὐλογητός ("blessed, praised") for barukh.

**11** Now [attah] I know [yadati] that YHWH [is] great [gadol] above all [mi-kol] the gods [ha-elohim]; indeed [ki], in the thing [ba-davar] in which they acted arrogantly [zadu] upon them [aleihem]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads עַתָּה יָדַעְתִּי כִּי־גָדוֹל יְהוָה מִכָּל־הָאֱלֹהִים כִּי בַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר זָדוּ עֲלֵיהֶם (attah yadati ki-gadol YHWH mi-kol-ha-elohim ki va-davar asher zadu aleihem, "now I know that YHWH [is] great above all the gods; indeed, in the thing in which they acted arrogantly upon them"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the greatest monotheistic confession by a non-Israelite:** עַתָּה (attah) = "now" — the adverb of present realization. This "now" marks a turning point in Yitro's theology: he has heard the evidence and reached a verdict. יָדַעְתִּי (yadati) = qal perfect 1cs of ידע (yada, "to know") — "I know, I have come to know." This is experiential, convicted knowledge — not abstract belief but the settled certainty that comes from weighing evidence. כִּי־גָדוֹל יְהוָה מִכָּל־הָאֱלֹהִים (ki-gadol YHWH mi-kol-ha-elohim) = "that YHWH [is] great above/beyond all the gods" — the comparative מִן (min) + כֹּל (kol, "all") + הָאֱלֹהִים (ha-elohim, "the gods"). This is not yet pure monotheism (there is only one God) but henotheism or monolatry (YHWH is supreme among gods). Yitro does not deny the existence of other gods; he declares YHWH greater than all of them. This confession is remarkable coming from a priest of Midyan — a man whose professional life was devoted to other deities. The second clause is notoriously obscure: כִּי בַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר זָדוּ עֲלֵיהֶם (ki va-davar asher zadu aleihem) = "for/indeed in the thing/matter in which they acted arrogantly against them." The verb זָדוּ (zadu) = qal perfect 3cp of זוד/זיד (zud/zid, "to act presumptuously, to be insolent, to boil over with arrogance"). The subject "they" is ambiguous: (a) the Egyptians acted arrogantly against Yisra'el, and YHWH punished them measure for measure (middah ke-neged middah) — they drowned Israelite babies in the Nile, so YHWH drowned them in the Yam Suf. This is Rashi's reading, following the Talmud (Sotah 11a): "In the very pot in which they cooked, they were cooked." (b) The "gods" acted arrogantly — the other gods who claimed power were shown to be powerless in the very domains where they boasted. The sentence is grammatically incomplete — it trails off, as if Yitro is so overwhelmed by the realization that he cannot finish. The Targumim and LXX supply various completions. LXX: νῦν ἔγνων ὅτι μέγας κύριος παρὰ πάντας τοὺς θεοὺς ἕνεκεν τούτου, ὅτι ἐπέθεντο αὐτοῖς ("now I know that the Lord is great beyond all the gods on account of this, that they attacked them") — the LXX renders zadu as ἐπιτίθημι ("set upon, attack") and supplies ἕνεκεν τούτου ("on account of this") to smooth the syntax.

**12** And Yitro, father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh, took [vayiqqakh] an olah [burnt-offering] and zevakhim [sacrifices] for Elohim [God]; and Aharon came [vayavo], and all [ve-khol] the elders [ziqnei] of Yisra'el, to eat [le-ekhol] lekhem [bread/food] with [im] the father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh before [lifnei] ha-Elohim [God].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקַּח יִתְרוֹ חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה עֹלָה וּזְבָחִים לֵאלֹהִים וַיָּבֹא אַהֲרֹן וְכֹל זִקְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֶאֱכָל־לֶחֶם עִם־חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה לִפְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (vayiqqakh Yitro khoten Mosheh olah u-zevakhim le-Elohim vayavo Aharon ve-khol ziqnei Yisra'el le-ekhol-lekhem im-khoten Mosheh lifnei ha-Elohim, "and Yitro, father-in-law of Mosheh, took a burnt-offering and sacrifices for God; and Aharon came, and all the elders of Yisra'el, to eat bread with the father-in-law of Mosheh before God"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — a Midyanite priest sacrifices to the God of Yisra'el:** וַיִּקַּח (vayiqqakh) = "and he took" — Yitro takes (procures, brings) sacrificial offerings. עֹלָה (olah) = "burnt-offering, ascent-offering" — from עלה (alah, "to go up, ascend"). The olah is wholly consumed on the altar; nothing is eaten by the worshiper. It represents total dedication to God. זְבָחִים (zevakhim) = "sacrifices, slaughter-offerings" — plural of זֶבַח (zevakh, "sacrifice"), from זבח (zavakh, "to slaughter, sacrifice"). Unlike the olah, the zevakh is a communion sacrifice where the worshiper eats a portion. This is why v. 12b mentions eating — the zevakhim provide the sacred meal. לֵאלֹהִים (le-Elohim) = "for God / to God." The remarkable thing is that Yitro — a non-Israelite priest — officiates or at least initiates sacrifice to the God of Yisra'el. Mosheh's conspicuous absence from the meal is noted by many commentators: the text says "Aharon came, and all the elders" but does not mention Mosheh. Was Mosheh serving the food? Was he standing in attendance? Rashi suggests Mosheh was serving them at table. לֶאֱכָל־לֶחֶם (le-ekhol-lekhem) = "to eat bread/food" — לֶחֶם (lekhem) here means "food" broadly, not just bread; it refers to the sacrificial meal. לִפְנֵי הָאֱלֹהִים (lifnei ha-Elohim) = "before God" — the meal is a sacred, covenantal act in God's presence. This is a covenant meal between Yisra'el's leadership and the Midyanite priest, ratifying the theological confession of vv. 10–11. LXX: καὶ ἔλαβεν Ιοθορ ὁ γαμβρὸς Μωυσῆ ὁλοκαυτώματα καὶ θυσίας τῷ θεῷ· παρεγένετο δὲ Ααρων καὶ πάντες οἱ πρεσβύτεροι Ισραηλ συμφαγεῖν ἄρτον μετὰ τοῦ γαμβροῦ Μωυσῆ ἐναντίον τοῦ θεοῦ ("and Iothor the father-in-law of Mosheh took whole-burnt-offerings and sacrifices for God; and Aaron and all the elders of Israel came to eat bread together with the father-in-law of Mosheh before God") — ὁλοκαύτωμα ("whole-burnt-offering") for olah; θυσία ("sacrifice") for zevakh; ἄρτος ("bread, food") for lekhem.

## The Judicial Reform (vv. 13–27)

**13** And it was [vayehi] on the next day [mi-makharat], and Mosheh sat [vayeshev] to judge [lishpot] the people [ha-am]; and the people stood [vaya'amod] over [al] Mosheh from the morning [min-ha-boqer] until [ad] the evening [ha-arev].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי מִמָּחֳרָת וַיֵּשֶׁב מֹשֶׁה לִשְׁפֹּט אֶת־הָעָם וַיַּעֲמֹד הָעָם עַל־מֹשֶׁה מִן־הַבֹּקֶר עַד־הָעָרֶב (vayehi mi-makharat vayeshev Mosheh lishpot et-ha-am vaya'amod ha-am al-Mosheh min-ha-boqer ad-ha-arev, "and it was on the next day, and Mosheh sat to judge the people; and the people stood over Mosheh from the morning until the evening"). **Key terms:** מִמָּחֳרָת (mi-makharat) = "on the next day, on the morrow" — from מָחָר (makhar, "tomorrow"). This places the judicial scene immediately after Yitro's arrival and the covenant meal. וַיֵּשֶׁב (vayeshev) = qal of ישׁב (yashav, "to sit, dwell") — "he sat." The judge sits; the litigants stand. Sitting is the posture of judicial authority (cf. Ps 122:5 — "there sat the thrones of judgment"). לִשְׁפֹּט (lishpot) = qal infinitive construct of שׁפט (shafat, "to judge, govern, decide disputes") — "to judge." The verb shafat encompasses the full range of judicial activity: hearing cases, rendering verdicts, establishing justice. וַיַּעֲמֹד (vaya'amod) = qal of עמד (amad, "to stand") — "stood." The people stand before the judge in a long queue. עַל (al) = "over, upon" — the people stood "over" Mosheh, suggesting they pressed around him, crowding in from all sides. The time frame min-ha-boqer ad-ha-arev ("from the morning until the evening") = an entire day, every day. The judicial burden is crushing and unsustainable. LXX: καὶ ἐγένετο μετὰ τὴν ἐπαύριον συνεκάθισεν Μωυσῆς κρίνειν τὸν λαόν· παρειστήκει δὲ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς Μωυσῇ ἀπὸ πρωίθεν ἕως ἑσπέρας ("and it came to be on the next day, Mosheh sat down to judge the people; and all the people stood beside Mosheh from morning until evening") — κρίνω ("judge") for shafat; παρίστημι ("stand beside") for vaya'amod al.

**14** And the father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh saw [vayar] all [et-kol] that he [was] doing [oseh] for the people [la-am], and he said: "What [mah] [is] this thing [ha-davar ha-zeh] that you [are] doing [oseh] for the people [la-am]? Why [maddua] do you [attah] sit [yoshev] alone [levaddekha], and all [ve-khol] the people stand [nitsav] over you [alekha] from morning [min-boqer] until [ad] evening [arev]?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּרְא חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה אֵת כָּל־אֲשֶׁר הוּא עֹשֶׂה לָעָם וַיֹּאמֶר מָה הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה לָעָם מַדּוּעַ אַתָּה יוֹשֵׁב לְבַדֶּךָ וְכָל־הָעָם נִצָּב עָלֶיךָ מִן־בֹּקֶר עַד־עָרֶב (vayar khoten Mosheh et kol-asher hu oseh la-am vayomer mah ha-davar ha-zeh asher attah oseh la-am maddua attah yoshev levaddekha ve-khol-ha-am nitsav alekha min-boqer ad-arev, "and the father-in-law of Mosheh saw all that he was doing for the people, and he said: 'What is this thing that you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand over you from morning until evening?'"). **Key terms:** מַדּוּעַ (maddua) = "why? for what reason?" — from מַה (mah, "what") + יָדוּעַ (yadua, "known"), literally "what is known?" = "for what known reason?" Yitro's question is practical and organizational, not theological. He sees an unsustainable system. לְבַדֶּךָ (levaddekha) = "alone, by yourself" — from בַּד (bad, "alone, separate, apart") + 2ms suffix. The key word: Mosheh is judging alone. This is the problem Yitro will solve. נִצָּב (nitsav) = niphal participle of יצב (yatsav, "to take one's stand, station oneself") — "standing, stationed." The same word used of Mosheh on the hilltop in 17:9 — now the people are the ones standing, immobile, waiting. LXX: καὶ ἰδὼν ὁ γαμβρὸς Μωυσῆ πάντα ὅσα ἐποίει τῷ λαῷ λέγει· τί τοῦτο ὃ σὺ ποιεῖς τῷ λαῷ; διὰ τί σὺ κάθησαι μόνος, πᾶς δὲ ὁ λαὸς παρέστηκέν σοι ἀπὸ πρωίθεν ἕως δείλης; ("and the father-in-law of Mosheh, seeing all the things he was doing for the people, says: 'What is this that you are doing for the people? Why do you sit alone, and all the people stand beside you from morning until late afternoon?'") — δείλη ("late afternoon, evening") for arev.

**15** And Mosheh said to his father-in-law [le-khotno]: "Because [ki] the people come [yavo] to me to inquire [lidrosh] of Elohim [God].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה לְחֹתְנוֹ כִּי־יָבֹא אֵלַי הָעָם לִדְרֹשׁ אֱלֹהִים (vayomer Mosheh le-khotno ki-yavo elai ha-am lidrosh Elohim, "and Mosheh said to his father-in-law: 'Because the people come to me to inquire of God'"). **Key terms:** לִדְרֹשׁ (lidrosh) = qal infinitive construct of דרשׁ (darash, "to seek, inquire, investigate, study") — "to seek, to inquire of." This is one of the most theologically rich verbs in Hebrew. דרשׁ means to seek YHWH's will, to consult the divine oracle, to study and interpret. The later Jewish tradition of midrash (מִדְרָשׁ) derives from this same root — midrash is the "seeking" of meaning in Scripture. Here the people come to Mosheh to seek God's decision on their disputes. Mosheh functions not merely as a secular judge but as a divine oracle — he mediates God's will to the people. LXX: καὶ λέγει Μωυσῆς τῷ γαμβρῷ· ὅτι παραγίνεται πρός με ὁ λαὸς ἐκζητῆσαι κρίσιν παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("and Mosheh says to his father-in-law: 'Because the people come to me to seek judgment from God'") — ἐκζητέω ("seek out, inquire") for lidrosh; κρίσις ("judgment, decision") is added — the LXX specifies "judgment" where the Hebrew simply says "to inquire of God."

**16** When [ki] a matter [davar] comes [yihyeh] to them [lahem], it comes [ba] to me, and I judge [ve-shafatti] between [bein] a man [ish] and his companion [u-vein re'ehu], and I make known [ve-hodati] the statutes [khuqqei] of ha-Elohim [God] and his torot [instructions/laws]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads כִּי־יִהְיֶה לָהֶם דָּבָר בָּא אֵלַי וְשָׁפַטְתִּי בֵּין אִישׁ וּבֵין רֵעֵהוּ וְהוֹדַעְתִּי אֶת־חֻקֵּי הָאֱלֹהִים וְאֶת־תּוֹרֹתָיו (ki-yihyeh lahem davar ba elai ve-shafatti bein ish u-vein re'ehu ve-hodati et-khuqqei ha-Elohim ve-et-torotav, "when a matter comes to them, it comes to me, and I judge between a man and his companion, and I make known the statutes of God and his torot [instructions]"). **Key terms:** דָּבָר (davar) = "word, matter, thing, case" — the most versatile noun in Hebrew. Here it means a legal case or dispute. וְשָׁפַטְתִּי (ve-shafatti) = qal perfect 1cs (with waw-consecutive) of שׁפט (shafat, "to judge") — "and I judge." וְהוֹדַעְתִּי (ve-hodati) = hiphil perfect 1cs of ידע (yada, "to know") — "and I make known, and I cause to know." Mosheh's judicial role has two dimensions: adjudicating between parties (ve-shafatti) and teaching divine law (ve-hodati). חֻקִּים (khuqqim) = "statutes, decrees, enactments" — plural of חֹק (khoq), from חקק (khaqaq, "to engrave, inscribe, decree"). These are the fixed, inscribed divine regulations. תּוֹרֹתָיו (torotav) = "his torot / his instructions/laws" — plural of תּוֹרָה (torah, "instruction, teaching, law, direction"), from ירה (yarah, "to throw, cast, direct, teach, instruct") + 3ms suffix. The plural torot is notable: multiple "instructions" or "teachings" — this suggests a body of divine instruction already existing before Sinai. The combination khuqqim + torot anticipates the fuller legal vocabulary of Sinai (cf. Lev 26:46; Deut 4:44–45). LXX: ὅταν γὰρ γένηταί τις αὐτοῖς ἀντιλογία καὶ ἔλθωσι πρός με, διακρίνω ἕκαστον καὶ συμβιβάζω αὐτοὺς τὰ προστάγματα τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τὸν νόμον αὐτοῦ ("for whenever they have a dispute and come to me, I decide for each one and I instruct them in the ordinances of God and his law") — ἀντιλογία ("dispute, controversy") for davar; διακρίνω ("decide, distinguish") for shafatti; συμβιβάζω ("instruct, prove, bring together") for hodati; πρόσταγμα ("ordinance, decree") for khuqqim; νόμος ("law") singular for torotav plural.

**17** And the father-in-law [khoten] of Mosheh said to him: "Not [lo] good [tov] [is] the thing [ha-davar] that you [are] doing [oseh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר חֹתֵן מֹשֶׁה אֵלָיו לֹא־טוֹב הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר אַתָּה עֹשֶׂה (vayomer khoten Mosheh elav lo-tov ha-davar asher attah oseh, "and the father-in-law of Mosheh said to him: 'Not good [is] the thing that you are doing'"). **Key terms:** לֹא־טוֹב (lo-tov) = "not good." This phrase echoes Gen 2:18 — לֹא־טוֹב הֱיוֹת הָאָדָם לְבַדּוֹ (lo-tov heyot ha-adam levaddo, "it is not good for the man to be alone"). Just as it was "not good" for Adam to be alone, it is "not good" for Mosheh to judge alone. The solution in both cases is the provision of helpers. Yitro speaks with the authority of a father-in-law and the wisdom of an experienced leader — his critique is direct and unqualified. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ ὁ γαμβρὸς Μωυσῆ πρὸς αὐτόν· οὐκ ὀρθῶς σὺ ποιεῖς τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο ("and the father-in-law of Mosheh said to him: 'You are not doing this thing rightly'") — ὀρθῶς ("rightly, correctly") for tov — the LXX shifts from "not good" (a quality of the thing) to "not rightly" (an adverb modifying the action).

**18** Wearing away you will wear away [navol tibbol] — both you [gam-attah] and also [gam] this people [ha-am ha-zeh] who [are] with you [immakh]; for the thing [ha-davar] [is] too heavy [kaved] for you [mimmekha]; you are not able [lo-tukhal] to do it [asotho] alone [levaddekha].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads נָבֹל תִּבֹּל גַּם־אַתָּה גַּם הָעָם הַזֶּה אֲשֶׁר עִמָּךְ כִּי־כָבֵד מִמְּךָ הַדָּבָר לֹא־תוּכַל עֲשֹׂהוּ לְבַדֶּךָ (navol tibbol gam-attah gam ha-am ha-zeh asher immakh ki-kaved mimmekha ha-davar lo-tukhal asotho levaddekha, "wearing away you will wear away — both you and also this people who [are] with you; for the thing [is] too heavy for you; you are not able to do it alone"). **Key terms:** נָבֹל תִּבֹּל (navol tibbol) = infinitive absolute + imperfect of נבל (naval, "to wither, fade, wear out, become foolish") — "you will surely wither / you will utterly wear out." The infinitive absolute construction intensifies: this is certain collapse. The root נבל is the same as נָבָל (naval, "fool, senseless person" — cf. 1 Sam 25:25) and נְבֵלָה (nevelah, "carcass, corpse"). The progression is from vitality to withering to death. Yitro's warning is not mild: you will destroy yourself and the people. כָבֵד (kaved) = "heavy, weighty, burdensome" — the same root as in 17:12 where Mosheh's hands were "heavy" (kvedim). The judicial burden is too heavy, just as the intercessory burden was too heavy. In both cases, others must share the load. גַּם...גַּם (gam...gam) = "both...and also" — the double gam emphasizes that both Mosheh and the people suffer from this arrangement. LXX: φθορᾷ καταφθαρήσῃ καὶ σὺ καὶ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς οὗτος ὅς ἐστιν μετὰ σοῦ· βαρύ σοι τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο, οὐ δυνήσῃ ποιεῖν μόνος ("with corruption you will be utterly corrupted, both you and all this people who are with you; this matter is heavy for you, you will not be able to do it alone") — φθορά ("corruption, destruction") and καταφθείρω ("utterly destroy, corrupt") for navol tibbol; βαρύς ("heavy") for kaved.

**19** Now [attah], listen [shema] to my voice [be-qoli]; I will counsel you [i'atskha], and may Elohim [God] be [vihi] with you [immakh]. Be [heyeh] you [attah] for the people [la-am] before [mul] ha-Elohim [God], and you [attah] shall bring [ve-heveita] the matters [ha-devarim] to ha-Elohim [God].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads עַתָּה שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי אִיעָצְךָ וִיהִי אֱלֹהִים עִמָּךְ הֱיֵה אַתָּה לָעָם מוּל הָאֱלֹהִים וְהֵבֵאתָ אַתָּה אֶת־הַדְּבָרִים אֶל־הָאֱלֹהִים (attah shema be-qoli i'atskha vihi Elohim immakh heyeh attah la-am mul ha-Elohim ve-heveita attah et-ha-devarim el-ha-Elohim, "now listen to my voice; I will counsel you, and may God be with you. Be you for the people before God, and you shall bring the matters to God"). **Key terms:** שְׁמַע בְּקֹלִי (shema be-qoli) = "listen to/in my voice" — the shema imperative followed by be-qol ("in the voice of") indicates obedient listening, heeding. אִיעָצְךָ (i'atskha) = qal imperfect 1cs of יעץ (ya'ats, "to counsel, advise") + 2ms suffix — "I will counsel you." The verb ya'ats is the root of יוֹעֵץ (yo'ets, "counselor") — one of the messianic titles in Isa 9:5 (pele yo'ets, "wonderful counselor"). Yitro functions here as a wise counselor. מוּל (mul) = "before, in front of, opposite" — Mosheh is to stand as the people's representative before God (mul ha-Elohim), the mediator who carries their cases into the divine presence. וְהֵבֵאתָ (ve-heveita) = hiphil perfect (waw-consecutive) of בוא (bo, "to come, bring") — "and you shall bring." Mosheh's specific role is to bring the difficult cases to God — to serve as the pipeline between human dispute and divine resolution. LXX: νῦν οὖν ἄκουσόν μου, καὶ συμβουλεύσω σοι, καὶ ἔσται ὁ θεὸς μετὰ σοῦ. γίνου σὺ τῷ λαῷ τὰ πρὸς τὸν θεὸν καὶ ἀνοίσεις τοὺς λόγους αὐτῶν πρὸς τὸν θεόν ("now then listen to me, and I will counsel you, and God will be with you. Be for the people the things toward God, and you shall bring their words to God") — the LXX adds αὐτῶν ("their") — "their words/matters."

**20** And you shall warn [ve-hizhartah] them [et-hem] [with] the statutes [ha-khuqqim] and the torot [instructions/laws], and you shall make known [ve-hodata] to them the way [ha-derekh] in which they shall walk [yelkhu], and the deed [ha-ma'aseh] which [asher] they shall do [ya'asun].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהִזְהַרְתָּה אֶתְהֶם אֶת־הַחֻקִּים וְאֶת־הַתּוֹרֹת וְהוֹדַעְתָּ לָהֶם אֶת־הַדֶּרֶךְ יֵלְכוּ בָהּ וְאֶת־הַמַּעֲשֶׂה אֲשֶׁר יַעֲשׂוּן (ve-hizhartah ethem et-ha-khuqqim ve-et-ha-torot ve-hodata lahem et-ha-derekh yelkhu vah ve-et-ha-ma'aseh asher ya'asun, "and you shall warn them [with] the statutes and the instructions, and you shall make known to them the way they shall walk in it, and the deed which they shall do"). **Key terms:** וְהִזְהַרְתָּה (ve-hizhartah) = hiphil perfect (waw-consecutive) of זהר (zahar, "to shine, warn, admonish") — "and you shall warn, and you shall admonish." The root zahar means "to shine, be bright," and in the hiphil "to warn, enlighten, admonish" — to make something shine forth, to illuminate the danger. The warning function is distinct from judging: Mosheh teaches proactively, not just reactively. הַדֶּרֶךְ (ha-derekh) = "the way, the road, the path" — from דרך (darakh, "to tread, walk, march"). "The way" becomes a major metaphor for faithful living (cf. Deut 5:33; Ps 1:1, 6; Prov 4:11). הַמַּעֲשֶׂה (ha-ma'aseh) = "the deed, the work, the action" — from עשׂה (asah, "to do, make"). Yitro outlines Mosheh's retained functions: (1) represent the people before God (v. 19); (2) bring cases to God (v. 19); (3) warn/teach the laws (v. 20); (4) show them the way of life and the deeds required (v. 20). Only the routine adjudication is delegated. LXX: καὶ διαμαρτυρῇ αὐτοῖς τὰ προστάγματα τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ τὸν νόμον αὐτοῦ καὶ σημανεῖς αὐτοῖς τὰς ὁδοὺς ἐν αἷς πορεύσονται ἐν αὐταῖς καὶ τὰ ἔργα ἃ ποιήσουσιν ("and you shall testify to them the ordinances of God and his law, and you shall show them the ways in which they shall walk in them, and the deeds which they shall do") — διαμαρτύρομαι ("testify solemnly, charge") for hizhartah; σημαίνω ("show, indicate") for hodata.

**21** And you [ve-attah] shall see [tekhezeh] from all [mi-kol] the people [ha-am] men [anshei] of khayil [valor/ability], fearers [yir'ei] of Elohim [God], men [anshei] of emet [truth/faithfulness], haters [son'ei] of unjust gain [batsa]; and you shall place [ve-samta] over them [aleihem] [as] commanders [sarei] of thousands [alafim], commanders [sarei] of hundreds [me'ot], commanders [sarei] of fifties [khamishim], and commanders [sarei] of tens [asarot].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאַתָּה תֶחֱזֶה מִכָּל־הָעָם אַנְשֵׁי־חַיִל יִרְאֵי אֱלֹהִים אַנְשֵׁי אֱמֶת שֹׂנְאֵי בָצַע וְשַׂמְתָּ עֲלֵהֶם שָׂרֵי אֲלָפִים שָׂרֵי מֵאוֹת שָׂרֵי חֲמִשִּׁים וְשָׂרֵי עֲשָׂרֹת (ve-attah tekhezeh mi-kol-ha-am anshei-khayil yir'ei Elohim anshei emet son'ei vatsa ve-samta aleihem sarei alafim sarei me'ot sarei khamishim ve-sarei asarot, "and you shall see from all the people men of valor, fearers of God, men of truth, haters of unjust gain; and you shall place over them commanders of thousands, commanders of hundreds, commanders of fifties, and commanders of tens"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the four qualifications and the four-tier system:** תֶחֱזֶה (tekhezeh) = qal imperfect of חזה (khazah, "to see, behold, perceive, have a vision") — "you shall see / you shall discern." The verb khazah is stronger than ראה (ra'ah, "to see"); it implies prophetic discernment, visionary perception — Mosheh must see with divine insight who is fit to judge. **The four qualifications:** (1) אַנְשֵׁי־חַיִל (anshei-khayil) = "men of valor / men of ability" — חַיִל (khayil) = "strength, power, wealth, ability, army, valor." These are men of proven competence and moral strength. (2) יִרְאֵי אֱלֹהִים (yir'ei Elohim) = "fearers of God" — from ירא (yare, "to fear, be in awe"). The fear of God is the foundational qualification for just judgment — one who fears God will not pervert justice for human advantage. (3) אַנְשֵׁי אֱמֶת (anshei emet) = "men of truth/faithfulness" — אֱמֶת (emet) = "truth, reliability, faithfulness," from אמן (aman, "to be firm, faithful"). Men whose word is trustworthy, whose character is reliable. (4) שֹׂנְאֵי בָצַע (son'ei vatsa) = "haters of unjust gain" — from שׂנא (sane, "to hate") + בֶּצַע (betsa, "gain, profit, unjust gain"), from בצע (batsa, "to cut off, break off, gain by violence"). These are men who despise bribery and corruption. **The four-tier system:** שָׂרֵי (sarei) = "commanders of, officers of, princes of" — from שַׂר (sar, "prince, commander, chief, ruler"). The four levels create a decimal military/judicial hierarchy: אֲלָפִים (alafim) = "thousands"; מֵאוֹת (me'ot) = "hundreds"; חֲמִשִּׁים (khamishim) = "fifties"; עֲשָׂרֹת (asarot) = "tens." This system appears again in Deut 1:15 and becomes the basis for Yisra'el's military and civic organization. LXX: καὶ σὺ σεαυτῷ σκέψαι ἀπὸ παντὸς τοῦ λαοῦ ἄνδρας δυνατοὺς θεοσεβεῖς ἄνδρας δικαίους μισοῦντας ὑπερηφανίαν καὶ καταστήσεις αὐτοὺς ἐπ᾽ αὐτῶν χιλιάρχους καὶ ἑκατοντάρχους καὶ πεντηκοντάρχους καὶ δεκαδάρχους ("and you yourself shall look out from all the people mighty men, God-fearing, righteous men, hating arrogance; and you shall set them over them as commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds and commanders of fifties and commanders of tens") — **LXX INTERPRETIVE SHIFTS:** δυνατός ("mighty, able") for anshei khayil; θεοσεβής ("God-fearing") for yir'ei Elohim; δίκαιος ("righteous, just") for anshei emet — the LXX renders "men of truth" as "righteous men"; μισοῦντας ὑπερηφανίαν ("hating arrogance/pride") for son'ei vatsa — a significant shift from "hating unjust gain" to "hating arrogance." χιλίαρχος / ἑκατόνταρχος / πεντηκόνταρχος / δεκάδαρχος = the Greek military-rank terms for the Hebrew four-tier system.

**22** And they shall judge [ve-shaftu] the people [et-ha-am] at all times [be-khol-et]; and it shall be [ve-hayah] — every great [ha-gadol] matter [ha-davar] they shall bring [yavi'u] to you, and every small [ha-qaton] matter [davar] they shall judge [yishpetu] — they [hem]. And lighten [ve-haqel] from upon you [me-alekha], and they shall bear [ve-nas'u] with you [ittakh].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְשָׁפְטוּ אֶת־הָעָם בְּכָל־עֵת וְהָיָה כָּל־הַדָּבָר הַגָּדֹל יָבִיאוּ אֵלֶיךָ וְכָל־הַדָּבָר הַקָּטֹן יִשְׁפְּטוּ הֵם וְהָקֵל מֵעָלֶיךָ וְנָשְׂאוּ אִתָּךְ (ve-shaftu et-ha-am be-khol-et ve-hayah kol-ha-davar ha-gadol yavi'u elekha ve-khol-ha-davar ha-qaton yishpetu hem ve-haqel me-alekha ve-nas'u ittakh, "and they shall judge the people at all times; and it shall be — every great matter they shall bring to you, and every small matter they shall judge — they. And lighten from upon you, and they shall bear with you"). **Key terms:** בְּכָל־עֵת (be-khol-et) = "at every time, always" — from עֵת (et, "time, appointed time"). The judges are available constantly, not just when Mosheh holds court. הַגָּדֹל (ha-gadol) = "the great, the large" and הַקָּטֹן (ha-qaton) = "the small, the little" — the distinction is between major/difficult cases (which go to Mosheh) and minor/routine cases (which the appointed judges handle). This is the principle of judicial hierarchy: escalation by difficulty. וְהָקֵל (ve-haqel) = hiphil imperative of קלל (qalal, "to be light, be swift, be trifling") — "and lighten! / make lighter!" — the opposite of כָבֵד (kaved, "heavy") from v. 18. The heavy burden is to be made light. וְנָשְׂאוּ (ve-nas'u) = qal perfect (waw-consecutive) of נשׂא (nasa, "to lift, carry, bear") — "and they shall bear/carry." The load is shared: "they shall bear with you" — not instead of you but alongside you. LXX: καὶ δικάσουσιν τῷ λαῷ πᾶσαν ὥραν· τὸ δὲ ῥῆμα τὸ ὑπέρογκον ἀνοίσουσιν ἐπὶ σέ, τὰ δὲ βραχέα τῶν κριμάτων κρινοῦσιν αὐτοί· καὶ κουφιοῦσιν ἀπὸ σοῦ καὶ συναντιλήμψονταί σοι ("and they shall judge the people at every hour; and the weighty matter they shall bring up to you, but the small judgments they shall judge themselves; and they shall lighten from you and share the burden with you") — ὑπέρογκος ("weighty, bulky, excessive") for gadol; βραχύς ("small, brief") for qaton; κουφίζω ("lighten") for haqel; συναντιλαμβάνομαι ("share in bearing, help together") for ve-nas'u ittakh.

**23** If [im] you do [ta'aseh] this thing [ha-davar ha-zeh], and Elohim [God] commands you [ve-tsivvekha], then you will be able [ve-yakholta] to stand [amod], and also [ve-gam] all [kol] this people [ha-am ha-zeh] — upon their place [al-meqomo] they shall come [yavo'u] in shalom [peace]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads אִם אֶת־הַדָּבָר הַזֶּה תַּעֲשֶׂה וְצִוְּךָ אֱלֹהִים וְיָכָלְתָּ עֲמֹד וְגַם כָּל־הָעָם הַזֶּה עַל־מְקֹמוֹ יָבֹא בְשָׁלוֹם (im et-ha-davar ha-zeh ta'aseh ve-tsivvekha Elohim ve-yakholta amod ve-gam kol-ha-am ha-zeh al-meqomo yavo ve-shalom, "if you do this thing, and God commands you, then you will be able to stand; and also all this people — upon their place they shall come in peace"). **Key terms:** וְצִוְּךָ אֱלֹהִים (ve-tsivvekha Elohim) = "and God commands you" — from צוה (tsivvah, "to command, charge, order"). This is a crucial qualification: Yitro's advice is conditional upon divine approval. Yitro does not presume to override YHWH — he says "if God commands you [to do this]." The human wisdom of administrative reform must be confirmed by divine sanction. וְיָכָלְתָּ עֲמֹד (ve-yakholta amod) = "and you will be able to stand" — from יכל (yakhol, "to be able, to prevail") + עמד (amad, "to stand"). "To stand" means to endure, to sustain the role without collapse. עַל־מְקֹמוֹ (al-meqomo) = "upon his/their place" — from מָקוֹם (maqom, "place, standing-place"), from קום (qum, "to rise, stand"). The people will go home to their places — disputes will be resolved efficiently and they will not have to stand all day in Mosheh's court. בְשָׁלוֹם (ve-shalom) = "in peace, in wholeness" — the outcome is shalom: not just the absence of conflict but the positive state of well-being, completeness, and social harmony. LXX: εἰ τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο ποιήσεις, κατισχύσει σε ὁ θεὸς καὶ δυνήσῃ παραστῆναι, καὶ πᾶς ὁ λαὸς οὗτος εἰς τὸν ἑαυτοῦ τόπον μετ᾽ εἰρήνης ἥξει ("if you do this thing, God will strengthen you and you will be able to stand, and all this people shall come to their own place with peace") — **LXX INTERPRETIVE SHIFT:** The LXX renders ve-tsivvekha Elohim ("and God commands you") as κατισχύσει σε ὁ θεός ("God will strengthen you") — a significant change from divine command/sanction to divine empowerment. εἰρήνη ("peace") for shalom.

LXX: καὶ ἐδίκαζον τὸν λαὸν πᾶσαν ὥραν· πᾶν δὲ ῥῆμα ὑπέρογκον ἀνεφέροσαν ἐπὶ Μωυσῆν, πᾶν δὲ ῥῆμα ἐλαφρὸν ἐκρίνοσαν αὐτοί ("and they judged the people at every hour; and every weighty matter they brought up to Mosheh, but every light matter they judged themselves") — ὑπέρογκος ("weighty, bulky") for qashah; ἐλαφρός ("light") for qaton.

**27** And Mosheh sent away [vayeshallakh] his father-in-law [khotno], and he went [vayelekh] for himself [lo] to his land [el-artso].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְשַׁלַּח מֹשֶׁה אֶת־חֹתְנוֹ וַיֵּלֶךְ לוֹ אֶל־אַרְצוֹ (vayeshallakh Mosheh et-khotno vayelekh lo el-artso, "and Mosheh sent away his father-in-law, and he went for himself to his land"). **Key terms:** וַיְשַׁלַּח (vayeshallakh) = piel of שׁלח (shalakh, "to send, send away") — "he sent away." The piel form suggests a deliberate, formal sending off — an honorable departure, not a dismissal. Compare the same root used for Tsipporah's "sending away" (shillukheha) in v. 2 — but Yitro's departure is voluntary and honored. לוֹ (lo) = "for himself / to himself" — the ethical dative, suggesting Yitro went of his own accord, at his own initiative. אֶל־אַרְצוֹ (el-artso) = "to his land" — from אֶרֶץ (erets, "land, earth, country") + 3ms suffix. Yitro returns to Midyan. The chapter began with Yitro coming from Midyan and ends with his return there. The narrative is a self-contained unit: arrival, confession, reform, departure. The question of whether Yitro ever fully joined Yisra'el is left unresolved here; Num 10:29–32 records Mosheh's later attempt to persuade Khovav (possibly Yitro or his son) to remain with Yisra'el as a wilderness guide. LXX: ἐξαπέστειλεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τὸν γαμβρὸν αὐτοῦ, καὶ ᾤχετο εἰς τὴν γῆν αὐτοῦ ("and Mosheh sent away his father-in-law, and he departed to his own land") — ἐξαποστέλλω ("send forth, send away") for vayeshallakh; οἴχομαι ("depart, go away") for vayelekh.

---

## Theological Summary

Exodus 18 presents a threefold revelation through the person of Yitro: (1) **The confession of a pagan priest** — Yitro's declaration in v. 11 (attah yadati ki-gadol YHWH mi-kol-ha-elohim, "now I know that YHWH is greater than all the gods") is the narrative fruit of the exodus: the plagues, the sea-crossing, and the wilderness provision have achieved their purpose of making YHWH known among the nations. (2) **The sacred meal** — a non-Israelite priest brings olah and zevakhim to the God of Yisra'el, and Yisra'el's elders eat bread "before God" with him (v. 12). This is covenantal fellowship crossing ethnic boundaries — Yitro is welcomed into the worship of YHWH. (3) **The wisdom of administrative delegation** — divine calling does not preclude practical restructuring. The four-tier judicial system (sarei alafim/me'ot/khamishim/asarot) establishes that YHWH's justice is mediated through human institutions, not solely through charismatic leaders. The chapter's placement between the wilderness wanderings (chs. 15–17) and the Sinai covenant (chs. 19–20) is deliberate: before YHWH gives the Torah, He first establishes the judicial infrastructure through which that Torah will be administered.
